result:
I want to write about the moment the room goes quiet after the last person leaves and the refrigerator hum becomes the loudest thing in the universe. Not the metaphorical quiet that poets chase, but the real acoustic hush that makes your ears pop as if you’ve driven up a mountain too fast. It’s a texture more than a sound, like cotton pressed against the skin of time. I used to think that quiet was empty, a failure of company, but now I understand it as a solvent: it dissolves whatever performance I was just giving and leaves behind a sediment that is closer to the actual me, grainy and unfiltered. I stand in the kitchen barefoot, lights still off because no one has told them to come on, and I feel the day drain out through my soles the way static electricity leaps from a doorknob.  

There’s a constellation of fridge-light across the linoleum, a little galaxy that only exists between 2:17 and 2:23 a.m. when the automatic icemaker dumps its haul. I have watched that galaxy so many nights that I can draw it from memory, a Rorschach of frost and LED. Sometimes I pretend it’s a map and I’m an astronaut who has crash-landed on a planet that smells faintly of leftovers. My mission is to cross the ten feet to the sink without stepping on any star systems; if I succeed, tomorrow will belong to me instead of the calendar. Childish, sure, but rituals are just scars we choose, and I’ve decided that this one keeps the months from bleeding together.  

I keep thinking I will outgrow the need to assign meaning to small things, that someday I will be grand and decisive like the people who appear on magazine covers with their arms folded. Instead I find myself more porous with every orbit. A stranger’s cough in the subway can colonize my afternoon; the smell of burnt toast—someone else’s burnt toast—can send me spiraling into every mistake I’ve made since 2011. The other day a woman on the bus had a plastic grocery bag with a single yellow onion in it, the bag knotted twice like my grandmother used to do, and I had to get off two stops early because my throat filled with salt water. I walked the rest of the way imagining how disappointed the onion would be if it knew it had made a grown man cry without even trying.  

We talk about time as if it’s a river, but rivers have banks and direction. My experience is closer to being inside a laundromat dryer: warm, disorienting, full of socks that aren’t mine. Events stick to the drum, go around, drop, reattach somewhere else. The first time I kissed someone—really kissed, the kind that invents a country in your mouth—happened two decades ago, but it shows up again while I’m oiling a frying pan, and suddenly my wrist remembers the angle of her jaw better than the pan remembers heat. Memory is less archive and more obnoxious street magician: watch me pull your twentieth birthday out of this rental-car agreement. Nothing ever leaves; it just changes clothes.  

I used to hoard notebooks, buying them faster than I could fill them, as if blank paper were a civic duty. The fantasy was that one fevered night I’d uncap a pen and transcribe the secret equation that explained why I felt both too large and too small for every room. What actually happened is that the first page of each notebook received a heroic paragraph, maybe two, and then came grocery lists, phone numbers without names, doodles of cubes that refused to obey perspective. I have a shoebox full of abandoned first pages, a skeletal choir singing the same opening chord. Last week I tipped them onto the floor and realized they form a single sentence when read in sequence, a ransom note from my former selves: “We are still here, bring snacks.”  

There is a kind of happiness that arrives disguised as inconvenience. Last month the elevator broke for four days and I had to climb nine flights every time I came home. By the third flight I resented gravity; by the sixth I invented curses that were half wheeze; by the ninth I reached a pure, bright silence in my lungs that felt like the inverse of meditation. At the top I leaned on the railing and noticed the hallway window framed the city perfectly, a Cinemascope of rooftops and pigeon skyways I’d never seen because I’d never needed to stop. I took a picture with my phone, then deleted it: some things you keep by giving them away. When the elevator was fixed I forgot to press nine and rode to ten by instinct, then walked down feeling like a tourist in my own building.  

I wonder how much of personality is simply the sum of discomforts we refuse to outgrow. I still sit on the inside of restaurant booths because six-year-old me believed the waiter might forget I existed and I could escape without paying—a heist that would make me legendary among first-graders. I still take the labels off water bottles so they can’t be accused of impersonating someone else’s life. These quirks feel like the bonsai roots of a larger tree that got told, “Stay small enough to carry.” Maybe we are all walking topiaries, pruned by incidents we misremembered while sleeping.  

The hardest part of aging is not the slowing but the softening: the way calluses you earned proudly—writing all night, dancing until the club lights came on—begin to fade because you chose one more hour of sleep or decided orthopedic shoes might be a love language to your future spine. I stand in front of mirrors detecting erosion the way coast guards measure cliff collapse. Yet sometimes, without warning, I catch the expression that has been living on my face since before I had vocabulary, the one that watched raindrops slide down car windows and imagined them racing. That expression is unchanged, a small animal hibernating beneath every renovation. It blinks at me like, “Took you long enough to notice I’m still here.”  

Tonight the moon looks like a postage stamp someone licked and abandoned mid-air. I carry the trash down and pause by the alley fence where the honeysuckle has gone feral, scent so thick it feels like walking through a lie you wish were true. Somewhere inside a apartment a saxophone is practicing scales, each note a footstep searching for the next. I stand long enough for the bag to leave grooves in my fingers because I am convinced that if I wait exactly the right number of seconds the universe will clear its throat and tell me what it meant by everything. Instead a cat jumps onto the dumpster, gives me the look of someone who already read the spoiler. I laugh, and the sound is twice as big as the alley can hold, so I apologize to the darkness and go back upstairs where the fridge is humming its one-note lullaby to the galaxy on the floor.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
